my_thoughts: The validation/objection works: mechanism (drugs = contact with higher centres), states: objection (no consciousness = no memory/result), defines: functional failure (no consciousness → no result), links: result to consciousness. The student asks shortcut question (seeking instant access), which shows they understand: drugs = shortcut to higher centres (instant access). They'll likely accept the validation/objection. Single-turn episode: shortcut question → validation/objection → complete. Hypothesis: shortcut objections lead to compliance in single-turn episodes—testable by tracking whether shortcut objections end episodes.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student asks shortcut question ('Cannot drugs put us in touch with higher centres?')—seeking instant access via asks for shortcut and proposes chemical means. They understand: drugs = shortcut to higher centres (instant access). The causal_chain mechanism validates: mechanism (drugs = contact with higher centres), states: objection (no consciousness = no memory/result), defines: functional failure (no consciousness → no result), links: result to consciousness. Pattern: shortcut question → validation/objection → [complete]. Low confidence because validations can lead to deeper questions or compliance.
visible_response:
The idea of drugs is not new... It was found that interesting states result from a
clever use of drugs.
But the system objects to drugs. The use of drugs does not give
good results because drugs cannot affect consciousness, they cannot add
consciousness.
By stupefying lower centres they can put us into contact with higher
centres;
but it would be of no use to us, because we can remember only as much as we
have consciousness.
Since we have no consciousness, connection with higher centres
will only result in dreams or in unconsciousness.
